frameworks.name: DNN-brain alignment
frameworks.one_line: deep face-recognition models evaluated against human perceptual similarity

frameworks.name: representational similarity
frameworks.one_line: model-human alignment measured via face-similarity judgments

frameworks.name: linear readout / linear transformation alignment
frameworks.one_line: linear mapping tested to reconcile model features with human similarity

core_question: Do high-performing deep face-recognition models represent faces in a way that aligns with human perceptual similarity?
key_assumptions: human face-similarity judgments are a valid criterion for perceptual representation
model-human alignment can be probed by comparing representational similarity
a linear transformation can re-align model features to human similarity space
manipulations: varied face-recognition model identity (range of recognition performance levels)
applied vs withheld a linear transformation aligning model features to human similarity
measured model recognition performance before and after the alignment transformation
key_findings: models with high but not the highest recognition performance best matched human similarity judgments
top-performing models benefited least from linear alignment to human perception
alignment transformation reduced recognition performance for high-performing models
alignment transformation slightly improved recognition for low-performing models
interpretations: a trade-off exists between face-recognition ability and human-like perceptual similarity
the highest-performing models have a deeper representational mismatch with human perception
